parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I keep remembering how effortless everything used to feel, like we just naturally fell into step without even trying. Now it feels like I am walking on eggshells every single time we are in the same room. I try to bring up small things, but the air just gets heavy and the conversation dies out completely. It has been dragging on like this for months, and I am just exhausted from constantly guessing what went wrong. I miss the easy laughter we used to share before everything got so complicated.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly draining to live in that space between how things used to be and how they are now. There is a specific kind of grief that comes with missing someone who is still right in front of you, but feels miles away.

When a relationship goes from "effortless" to "walking on eggshells," it creates a constant state of hyper-vigilance. You’re likely spending so much energy analyzing your words, their tone, and the silence between you that you don’t have any energy left for yourself. That "heavy air" you described is usually the weight of all the things that aren't being said.

After months of this, it makes total sense that you are exhausted. Guessing is a lonely game, and it’s one you can’t actually win because you’re trying to solve a puzzle when the other person has half the pieces hidden.

Have you reached a point where you feel like you can address the "heaviness" itself, rather than the small things? Sometimes, when the small talk is dying, the only thing left to talk about is the silence. Saying something like, *"I feel like we’re both walking on eggshells, and I miss how easy it used to be between us,"* is terrifying, but it at least stops the guessing.

Regardless of what happens next, please acknowledge how much work you’ve been putting in just to keep things afloat. It’s okay to be tired of carrying the weight of the conversation all by yourself.

turns.user_message: The silence between us has stretched into something so thick that I barely recognize the person I used to know. I find myself replaying old memories just to remind myself that we actually used to be this close. Every attempt to fix the distance just ends up highlighting how far apart we really are now. I am tired of pretending that everything is fine when the tension is practically vibrating off the walls. It feels like I am holding onto a ghost of what we had while the reality just keeps slipping away.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly draining to live in that space between what a relationship used to be and what it has become. There is a specific kind of grief that comes with mourning someone who is still physically there, but emotionally unreachable.

When the silence gets that "thick," it often feels like you’re speaking two different languages, or like every word you try to use to bridge the gap just becomes another brick in the wall. Replaying those old memories is a natural survival tactic—it’s your mind trying to find solid ground—but it also makes the current reality feel even colder by comparison.

That "vibrating tension" you mentioned is exhausting because it requires you to be constantly on guard. You’re essentially grieving the relationship while simultaneously trying to perform the role of being "okay," and no one can sustain that forever without burning out.

It sounds like you are reaching a point of emotional exhaustion where the effort of holding onto the "ghost" is starting to outweigh the hope of bringing the relationship back to life. When you look at the distance between you now, does it feel like a gap that *can't* be closed, or does it feel like you’re the only one still trying to hold the rope?

turns.user_message: I look at the space between us and it feels like an entire lifetime has passed since we actually understood each other. The easy rhythm we once shared has completely shattered into this exhausting cycle of walking on eggshells and second-guessing every single word. I keep waiting for a spark of the old warmth to return, but it just feels like I am staring at a locked door. This heavy ache has settled so deep in my chest that I barely remember what it feels like to just breathe easy around them. I am just so incredibly tired of mourning something that is still technically right in front of me.
turns.model_response:
There is a specific, exhausting kind of grief that comes from mourning someone who is still sitting right across from you. It’s often lonelier than a clean break because the physical presence of the person serves as a constant reminder of the emotional absence of the connection you used to have.

That "heavy ache" you’re describing sounds like the weight of constant vigilance. When a relationship shifts from an easy rhythm to a "cycle of walking on eggshells," your nervous system never gets to rest. You are essentially in survival mode, scanning for threats or misunderstandings in a place that used to be your sanctuary. It makes sense that you feel like you can’t breathe; it’s hard to take a full breath when you’re bracing for impact.

It is incredibly draining to be the one holding the "key" and trying it in a door that won't budge. It leaves you wondering if the lock was changed, or if the person on the other side has simply moved away from the door entirely.

When you look at them now, do you feel like you are looking at the person they are today, or are you mostly searching for the ghost of who they used to be? Sometimes the hardest part of this "mourning" is realizing that the version of them you understood so well might not exist in the same way anymore.

Please be gentle with yourself. Feeling "incredibly tired" isn't just a lack of sleep—it’s the soul-weariness that comes from trying to bridge a gap that keeps getting wider. You are carrying a lot of weight right now.
